items.prompt_md: Why does the level-order BFS snapshot `level_size = len(queue)` **before** the inner `for` loop, instead of just looping `while queue:` at the inner level too?
options: It freezes how many nodes belong to the current level, before their children get enqueued into the same queue
It avoids an infinite loop, since the queue would otherwise never empty
It sorts the nodes within a level by value
It reduces the algorithm's time complexity from O(n^2) to O(n)
items.answer: It freezes how many nodes belong to the current level, before their children get enqueued into the same queue
items.explanation_md: The inner loop enqueues the next level's children as it runs, so without snapshotting the count first, there would be no way to tell where the current level ends and the next one begins.

items.prompt_md: What container does level-order BFS use to track which nodes to visit next, and why does that specific container matter?
options: A FIFO queue — nodes are processed in the order they were discovered, so one whole depth finishes before the next begins
A LIFO stack — the most recently discovered node is processed first
A hash set — only distinct node values are tracked
A sorted array — nodes are processed in value order
items.answer: A FIFO queue — nodes are processed in the order they were discovered, so one whole depth finishes before the next begins
items.explanation_md: FIFO order is what guarantees everything discovered at depth d is popped before anything discovered at depth d + 1 — a stack (LIFO) would instead dive depth-first, which is exactly what DFS does.

items.prompt_md: In the level-order loop, what should happen if `root` is `None` before the `while queue` loop even starts?
options: Return an empty list immediately, since there are no levels to build
Push `None` onto the queue and let the loop handle it
Raise an error, since level-order requires a non-empty tree
Start the queue with an empty list instead of `[root]`
items.answer: Return an empty list immediately, since there are no levels to build
items.explanation_md: An empty tree has zero levels, so the function should short-circuit and return `[]` rather than trying to seed the queue with `None`.

items.prompt_md: Inside the inner `for _ in range(level_size)` loop, in what order should a node's children be pushed onto the queue?
options: Left before right, so the next level is discovered left-to-right
Right before left, so the next level is discovered right-to-left
Order doesn't matter, since BFS ignores left/right structure
Children should never be pushed — only popped
items.answer: Left before right, so the next level is discovered left-to-right
items.explanation_md: Enqueuing left before right preserves left-to-right order within the next level, matching how the level's list is expected to read.

items.prompt_md: A student writes level-order BFS but checks `while queue and len(result) < max_levels` using a plain `while queue:` for the inner loop too (no `level_size` snapshot). What bug results?
options: Nodes from the next level get mixed into the current level's list, because children are enqueued and then immediately dequeued within the same inner loop
The queue never empties, causing an infinite loop
The tree's root is skipped entirely
Nothing breaks — the snapshot is only a minor optimization
items.answer: Nodes from the next level get mixed into the current level's list, because children are enqueued and then immediately dequeued within the same inner loop
items.explanation_md: Without freezing the current level's size first, the inner loop has no boundary and will keep popping newly-enqueued next-level nodes as if they belonged to the current level.

items.prompt_md: How would you adapt level-order BFS to return only the **last** node of each level (a right-side view), without necessarily building each full level's list?
options: Keep the same queue/level_size structure, but only append the value of the last node processed in each inner loop iteration
Switch to DFS with an explicit stack, since BFS cannot access the last node of a level
Reverse the queue before processing each level
Use two queues, one for left children and one for right
items.answer: Keep the same queue/level_size structure, but only append the value of the last node processed in each inner loop iteration
items.explanation_md: The level_size snapshot already isolates one level per outer iteration; you just record the value on the final iteration of the inner loop (or equivalently, the first if scanning right-to-left) instead of collecting the whole level.

items.prompt_md: Why is BFS, not DFS, the natural fit for 'find the fewest hops from the root to any node' in an unweighted tree?
options: BFS discovers nodes in strict order of distance from the root, so the first time a node is reached is guaranteed to be via a shortest path
DFS cannot visit every node in a tree
BFS uses less memory than DFS in every case
BFS and DFS give identical results for this question, so either works
items.answer: BFS discovers nodes in strict order of distance from the root, so the first time a node is reached is guaranteed to be via a shortest path
items.explanation_md: Because BFS empties the queue in FIFO order, everything at distance d is dequeued before anything at distance d + 1 — a guarantee DFS's depth-first recursion doesn't give you.

items.prompt_md: You could try to build level-grouped output with DFS instead of BFS by recursing with a `depth` parameter and appending into `result[depth]`. What extra bookkeeping does this DFS approach require that BFS avoids?
options: Pre-allocating (or growing) `result` to have a bucket for depths not yet reached, since DFS may dive deep into one branch before visiting shallower ones elsewhere
DFS cannot track depth at all, so this approach is impossible
DFS requires a queue anyway, so there is no real difference
BFS also needs the same depth-bucket bookkeeping
items.answer: Pre-allocating (or growing) `result` to have a bucket for depths not yet reached, since DFS may dive deep into one branch before visiting shallower ones elsewhere
items.explanation_md: A DFS recursion can reach a deep node in one branch long before finishing shallower nodes in another, so it has to grow buckets out of order — BFS's queue gives you levels already in order, for free.

items.prompt_md: What is the very first thing enqueued before the `while queue:` loop begins in level-order BFS?
options: The root node itself, e.g. `queue = deque([root])`
The root's left and right children
An empty list representing level 0
Nothing — the queue starts empty and is filled inside the loop
items.answer: The root node itself, e.g. `queue = deque([root])`
items.explanation_md: BFS needs a starting point in the queue before the loop can process anything, so the root seeds the queue before the first iteration.

items.prompt_md: Why is `collections.deque` preferred over a plain Python list for the BFS queue?
options: `deque.popleft()` removes from the front in O(1), while `list.pop(0)` requires shifting every remaining element — O(n)
A `deque` can only store tree nodes, unlike a list
A `deque` automatically sorts its contents
There's no real difference; either works equally well
items.answer: `deque.popleft()` removes from the front in O(1), while `list.pop(0)` requires shifting every remaining element — O(n)
items.explanation_md: Using `list.pop(0)` inside the BFS loop would turn each dequeue into an O(n) operation, degrading the whole traversal to O(n^2); `deque` keeps dequeuing O(1).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of level-order BFS on an n-node binary tree?
options: O(n) — every node is enqueued and dequeued exactly once
O(n log n) — the queue must be kept sorted
O(n^2) — each level rescans all previously seen nodes
O(h) — only the tree's height matters, not its total size
items.answer: O(n) — every node is enqueued and dequeued exactly once
items.explanation_md: Each of the n nodes enters and leaves the queue exactly one time, with O(1) work per node, so the total is O(n).

items.prompt_md: What is the worst-case space complexity of level-order BFS's queue, and when is it reached?
options: O(n), reached when the widest level holds close to n/2 nodes (a wide, bottom-heavy tree)
O(h), the same bound as recursive DFS's call stack
O(1), since only one node is dequeued at a time
O(log n), assuming the tree is perfectly balanced
items.answer: O(n), reached when the widest level holds close to n/2 nodes (a wide, bottom-heavy tree)
items.explanation_md: Unlike DFS's O(h) call stack, the BFS queue can hold an entire level at once — and the widest level of a complete binary tree is close to n/2 nodes, giving O(n) space in the worst case.

items.prompt_md: You need to return the tree's nodes grouped row by row, from the root's level downward. Which approach fits?
options: BFS with a FIFO queue, snapshotting the queue length at the start of each level
Post-order DFS recursion that returns a combined value per node
A hash map keyed by node value, checked for membership as you scan
Two pointers converging from the tree's two deepest leaves
items.answer: BFS with a FIFO queue, snapshotting the queue length at the start of each level
items.explanation_md: 'Grouped by row/depth' is the BFS tell. A queue naturally discovers nodes in level order, and snapshotting its length isolates one level per iteration of the outer loop.

items.prompt_md: You need to return only the rightmost node visible at each depth of a binary tree, as if looking at it from the right side. Which approach fits?
options: BFS with a queue, recording only the last node processed on each level
Post-order recursion returning a single aggregate boolean
A hash map keyed by node depth, storing the smallest value seen
Two pointers, one starting at the root and one at the deepest leaf
items.answer: BFS with a queue, recording only the last node processed on each level
items.explanation_md: 'What do you see from the side' is a per-level question, so BFS with the level_size snapshot fits — you just keep the last node of each level instead of the whole row.

items.prompt_md: You need the minimum number of edges from the root to the *nearest* leaf in a binary tree. Which approach fits?
options: BFS with a queue, stopping and returning as soon as the first leaf is dequeued
DFS recursion that always explores the left subtree fully before the right
A hash map keyed by node value, tracking depth as a side value
Two pointers moving from the root at different speeds
items.answer: BFS with a queue, stopping and returning as soon as the first leaf is dequeued
items.explanation_md: 'Fewest hops' in an unweighted tree is BFS's signature — the first leaf dequeued is guaranteed to be at the minimum depth, which DFS can't guarantee without exploring the whole tree first.

items.prompt_md: You need to compute the average value of the nodes at each depth of a binary tree, one average per level. Which approach fits?
options: BFS with a queue, summing and counting the nodes captured within one level_size snapshot
Post-order recursion that returns one combined average for the whole tree
A hash map keyed by node value, storing running sums
Two pointers converging inward across the tree's leaves
items.answer: BFS with a queue, summing and counting the nodes captured within one level_size snapshot
items.explanation_md: 'One result per level' is exactly what the level_size-snapshotted BFS loop naturally produces — sum and count within each level's inner loop, then divide.

items.prompt_md: You need to connect each node in a binary tree to its immediate right neighbor **on the same level** (a 'next right pointer'), level by level. Which approach fits?
options: BFS with a queue, linking consecutive nodes as they're dequeued within the same level_size snapshot
Post-order DFS recursion returning a combined node reference
A hash map keyed by node depth, storing only the deepest node
Two pointers scanning the tree's in-order sequence
items.answer: BFS with a queue, linking consecutive nodes as they're dequeued within the same level_size snapshot
items.explanation_md: Same-level adjacency is discovered naturally in BFS's dequeue order — consecutive pops within one level_size snapshot are exactly the nodes that should be linked to each other.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the length of the shortest path between two given nodes in an unweighted, undirected graph (not necessarily a tree). Which approach fits?
options: BFS with a queue, expanding outward one hop at a time from the start node
Post-order aggregate recursion returning a combined distance
A hash map keyed by edge weight
Two pointers starting at the two given nodes and moving toward each other
items.answer: BFS with a queue, expanding outward one hop at a time from the start node
items.explanation_md: Shortest path in an unweighted graph is the same BFS idea taught here — a queue discovers nodes in strict order of distance, so the target is first reached via a shortest path.

items.prompt_md: You need to merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted linked list, in place, using only pointer manipulation. Which approach fits?
options: Walk both lists with pointers, splicing nodes onto a dummy-headed result list in sorted order
BFS with a queue, dequeuing one node per list per level
A hash map keyed by node value, deduplicating as you go
Bottom-up aggregate recursion returning a combined length
items.answer: Walk both lists with pointers, splicing nodes onto a dummy-headed result list in sorted order
items.explanation_md: Two linked lists with no branching or level structure call for the linked-list dummy-head merge pattern, not a queue-based tree traversal.

items.prompt_md: You need to evaluate a fully-parenthesized arithmetic expression given as a string, respecting nested parentheses. Which approach fits?
options: A stack that pushes operators and operands, resolving parenthesized groups as closing brackets are matched
BFS with a queue, evaluating one 'level' of the expression at a time
Bottom-up aggregate recursion over the tree's levels
Two pointers scanning the expression from both ends
items.answer: A stack that pushes operators and operands, resolving parenthesized groups as closing brackets are matched
items.explanation_md: Nested-parenthesis evaluation over a flat string is the stack pattern's signature — there's no tree here for BFS or DFS to traverse.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine whether a binary tree is a **complete** binary tree (every level full except possibly the last, filled left-to-right). Which approach fits?
options: BFS with a queue, checking that no non-null node is dequeued after a null gap has been seen
Post-order recursion returning only a subtree sum
A hash map keyed by node value, tracking duplicates
Two pointers scanning the tree's leaves from left and right
items.answer: BFS with a queue, checking that no non-null node is dequeued after a null gap has been seen
items.explanation_md: Completeness is about left-to-right fill order across levels, which BFS's discovery order (including enqueuing `None` placeholders for missing children) checks directly.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the maximum width of a binary tree — the largest number of nodes across any single level, counting gaps from missing nodes. Which approach fits?
options: BFS with a queue, tracking each node's positional index within its level to measure the span between the first and last node
Post-order recursion returning a single aggregate integer with no positional info
A hash map keyed by node value
Two pointers converging from the tree's leftmost and rightmost leaves directly
items.answer: BFS with a queue, tracking each node's positional index within its level to measure the span between the first and last node
items.explanation_md: 'Widest level' is fundamentally a per-level measurement, so BFS with the level_size snapshot (plus tracking positional index) is the fit — pure post-order aggregation has no notion of horizontal position.

items.prompt_md: You are given a sorted array and need to find the index of a target value in O(log n) time. Which approach fits?
options: Binary search, repeatedly halving the search range
BFS with a queue, treating the array as if it were tree levels
Bottom-up aggregate recursion over array segments
An explicit stack pushing array indices
items.answer: Binary search, repeatedly halving the search range
items.explanation_md: A sorted array with a target-index question calls for narrowing a search range from both ends inward (binary search's two-pointer-style shrinking window), not any tree traversal.

items.prompt_md: You need the single deepest level's node count in a binary tree, without needing any other level's data. Which approach fits?
options: BFS with a queue, keeping only the size of the last level_size snapshot before the queue empties
Post-order recursion returning the tree's total node count
A hash map keyed by node value, storing depth as the value
Two pointers converging from the two deepest leaves
items.answer: BFS with a queue, keeping only the size of the last level_size snapshot before the queue empties
items.explanation_md: Even when only the final level matters, BFS's level_size snapshot naturally exposes each level's count — just keep the most recent one instead of recording every level.
